item.title: Re: Hesitant +1
item.content:encoded:
It is an antimemetic infovore, pretty much!

I enjoyed thinking about the various ways something inhuman would process information, how certain forms of communication might appear uniform to It- as a raw data stream.

There is some funky timeline stuff happening as well that i don't believe anyone has mentioned yet but essentially

spoiler
-

Knowledge of 3959's existence was wiped from their memories at least twice during the course of the article.

They created it, it got out of hand, they forgot they created it. They thought they discovered something new, and then they forgot about it again.

How many times could it have happened if the terminal was not found?

item.title: Hesitant +1
item.content:encoded:
I'm kinda curious if I'm actually following this right, since so many people are saying that it confuses them.

+ Spoilers
- REDACTED

So isn't this just a rouge anti-meme? Like, it's a foundation device that automatically redacts classified information, but the story's that it's redacting everything, from street signs to foundation documents to memories to the information presented by the mirror, that being whoever is shown in the reflection.

But yeah, it's a hesitant +1 mostly because I think without all the gimmicks, it's a fairly simple concept. You execute it really, really well though, and that's why this is a +1.
Wow! thanks everyone for their support

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pretty good. I don't think I 100% understood what all was happening, but I still liked it enough to +1 it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Safari on an iPad

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It redirects to http://www.scp-wiki.net/scp-3959-restricted

What browser are you running?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Once I entered the password,nothing happened except a white box which said “please wait….” Was anything else suppose to happen?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Scroll down to the bottom of the page. There's hidden text in the last box.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So what's the password to the computer on the page?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No longer apollyon. Was only for a little while, at another's suggestion

I would debate that whether or not the use of the classification is warranted ultimately comes down to two factors:

  • The inability to stop the anomaly
  • The impending K-Class

In this case, 3125 simply chose not to be classified as Apollyon, where it wouldn't go amiss if it were.

As far as this being 3002… i honestly have no idea how you make that comparison. They are almost polar opposites in fact

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: He just edited it. He originally had it as 2 ps and one l.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Apollyon wasn't misspelled.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In terms of both threat and quality this does not merit an Apollyon classification. (Which you misspelled btw)

Moreover, it feels extremely derivative of 3002. Same themes, same type of threat, same scale of threat. (albeit that one rightly does not use Apollyon even though the threat is arguably greater than in this piece)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh…..
Oh boy, that is pretty bad.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
yeah, i was okay with it being at the bottom left because it made it stand out more, but forcing it over the flashing cursor is quite pretty. Should have done that to begin with tbh

Originally, in my sandbox, it was all handled within the canvas, I had to getcharcode and have it respond accordingly with backspace/shift/enter.

It worked for the most part but the wiki has some sort of extension that prevents javascript from opening an embedded page through use of external code scripting.

Then I made it so that entering the passcode opened up a hidden div, but the form would only allow me to expose the hidden div ABOVE the terminal. I really didn't want to iron out a z-index, so I just scaled it back and made it simple.

Thanks for the help though!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay, you read the supplement, yes?

Think of it like this:

How did it react when exposed to a painting?

The original exposure to a mirror was a termination attempt.

Mirrors are a form of visual communication. Essentially they tried to kill it by exposing it to itself.

Instead of killing it, it treated the mirror like the painting and erased all data in the mirror. So, the knowledge (and possibly the existence) of everything in the mirror's reflection, gone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Look, I do not understand. I'm very sorry. But can you explain it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1 GOTO 2
2 GOTO 1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Still needs the "top:300px;left:410px;" part for the password input- that's the part that forces it to display in a particular place rather than off in margin hyperspace.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm confused.
What did exposing a mirror do to it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah that was my issue. It was there, but hidden on the right; I could only see it by hitting Tab a bunch.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks, I'll implement that when I'm back at my computer. Tbh I probably shouldn't have used a canvas at all. Didn't think there would be so many issues on other browsers.

Edit: compiled fine, so I'm under the assumption that all is well (still can't tell the difference on chrome)

I might have to resize it for mobile unfortunately

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Working on a fix for you, almost got it…

…okay, in the hidden stuff part, right below

 <canvas style="position:absolute;width:800px;height:400px;"></canvas>

change those lines to

 <input id="password" input="" style="position:absolute;width:200;height:100;top:300px;left:410px;background:transparent;color:#7CFC00;border: solid 1px; border-color:#7CFC00" type="text"> <input value="" name="submit" style="position:absolute;top:300px;left:560px;background:transparent;border:none;" type="submit">

You can change "border:none;" or the background color on the second one if you don't want the submit button to be invisible.

Explanation: At least for me the problem was that the password thingy was trying to appear in various places around the canvas box instead of inside it or below it. (In my case, it was hidden in HTML hyperspace to the right of the box, and only appeared when I tab-ed around the page, which forced the container to scroll over to it.) The tweaks lock it and the submit button so that they're next to the flashing cursor, and formats them to look sort of like they belong there for minimal disruption while also making it semi-obvious what you're supposed to do.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: yup. Should I make it bigger? Also some people seem to be having JS canvas issues on some other browsers. Unless I can make it universal, it might have to go.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Use the little white box by the bottom left of black box at the top.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's no place to enter the passcode.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Back with something I've been toying with for a long time.

I'll be back to clean up my code later, but all is functioning.

Formatting is a pain
